nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving freshwater rivers, streams, and flooded forest margins; heavily vegetated banks with overhanging riparian vegetation from which it hunts insects

decorPreferences: tight-fitting or overhanging lid (mandatory — confirmed jumper)
overhanging riparian plants or artificial foliage at water surface for hunting simulation
driftwood and smooth rocks along margins
dim to moderate lighting to reduce surface reflection stress
robust or floating plants (water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) at surface

diet.notes: Insectivore-leaning carnivore. Wild diet consists almost entirely of insects and other arthropods knocked from overhanging vegetation by precisely aimed jets of water. In captivity, offer live or frozen crickets, mealworms, waxworms, fruit flies (Drosophila), and feeder insects placed at or above the water surface to trigger the spitting reflex. Will accept frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and krill at the surface; can be trained to accept floating carnivore pellets or freeze-dried insects, but live/fresh insect prey should remain the dietary centrepiece. Feed small portions 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: similarly sized peaceful to semi-aggressive surface fish
bigger characins tolerant of warmer, neutral-alkaline water
freshwater gobies (bottom-dwellers that avoid the surface)
robust mid-water cichlids of comparable size
avoidWith: small fish that will be eaten or harassed (tetras, rasboras, nano species)
fin-nippers that may target archerfish flanks
invertebrates — shrimp and small snails risk being eaten
T. jaculatrix (Banded Archerfish) — that species requires brackish; mixing causes husbandry conflict
conspecific juveniles significantly smaller than resident adults (cannibalism risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo summary page for Toxotes blythii (species ID 6610). Verify individual image licensing before use.
Wikimedia category page for T. blythii; check for CC-licensed images contributed by aquarists or field researchers.

summary: The Clouded Archerfish (Toxotes blythii) is one of the few true freshwater archerfishes from Myanmar and Thailand — unlike most Toxotes, it requires no brackish salt. It shares the family’s signature behaviour: spitting precise jets of water to knock insects from overhanging vegetation. Grows to 12–18 cm; advanced care.
careNotes: T. blythii is a specialist surface predator that stands out in the Toxotidae family as a confirmed true freshwater species; it should not be kept with salt additions that benefit T. jaculatrix and most other archerfishes. A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is generally recommended to accommodate a small shoal (3+) and provide adequate open-surface hunting territory. A tightly fitting lid is non-negotiable — archerfish are powerful, determined jumpers and will exploit any gap. Leave a small air gap of a few centimetres between the water surface and the lid to allow the fish to “shoot” at insects placed on the underside of the lid or clipped to overhanging decor, which enriches the animals and encourages natural behaviour. Maintain neutral to slightly alkaline freshwater (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 8–20) and warm temperatures (24–28°C). Water quality must be high; archerfish are messy eaters and a large sump or efficient canister filtration with regular water changes is advised. Conspecific aggression within a shoal is typically low but group members will establish a loose hierarchy; keep 3 or more to distribute dominance behaviour. Archerfish are highly visual hunters — avoid keeping them with small fish that fit in their mouths. The species is rarely kept and infrequently traded; sourcing may require specialist importers. Because T. blythii remains poorly documented in captivity compared to T. jaculatrix, husbandry parameters are partly inferred from congeners and the limited published record.
breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding records for T. blythii are known as of 2026. In the wild, archerfish are presumed broadcast spawners with pelagic eggs; captive reproduction has not been achieved for any Toxotes species in a home aquarium context. Breeding in captivity should be considered currently not achievable for the home hobbyist.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Toxotes blythii
primary.notes: Primary source for species identity, native range (Irrawaddy drainage, Myanmar; Mae Klong drainage, Thailand), freshwater-only status, adult size (to ~17 cm), water parameters, habitat notes, and distinction from T. jaculatrix.

additional.name: FishBase — Toxotes blythii
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Toxotidae, Perciformes), adult size (reported to 20 cm TL in wild specimens; 18 cm used as aquarium maximum), native range (freshwater rivers of Myanmar and Thailand), temperature range (24–28°C), and freshwater classification. FishBase species ID 6610.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Archerfish species guide
additional.notes: Species comparison article noting T. blythii as the freshwater congener distinguished from the brackish T. jaculatrix. Supplemented with care notes on shooting behaviour, lid requirements, and insect-prey diet for the genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Archerfish care
additional.notes: General archerfish husbandry guidance (tank size, feeding strategy, jumping risk, group size). Notes that most archerfish in trade are T. jaculatrix; T. blythii freshwater status highlighted as unusual. Applied genus-level care guidance where species-specific data was absent.

additional.name: Kottelat, M. (1998) — Fishes of the Nam Theun and Xe Bangfai basins (Ichthyological Exploration of Freshwaters)
additional.notes: Ichthyological reference confirming T. blythii as a freshwater species within Southeast Asian drainages; cited via FishBase reference record. Used to support freshwater waterType classification and native-range countries.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented for T. blythii or any Toxotes species in a home aquarium. Presumed broadcast spawner in the wild. Not achievable by hobbyists with current knowledge.
